1: <header>Environmental life-cycle assessment of various dairy products </header>

Ilija Djekic a, Jelena Miocinovic Igor Tomasevic b Nada Smigic Nikola Tomic a a Department of Food Safety and Quality Management, University of Belgrade, Faculty of Agriculture, Belgrade, Serbia b Department of Animal Origin Products Technology, University of Belgrade, Faculty of Agriculture, Belgrade, Serbia
2: Article history: Received 16 September 2013 Received in revised form 19 December 2013 Accepted 22 December 2013 Available online 28 December 2013
Keywords: Life-cycle assessment Dairy products Environment Indicators Footprint
3: The objective of this paper was to assess the environmental performance of the production of six types of dairy products/pasteurized and ultra-high temperature (UHT) milk, yoghurt, cream, butter and cheese/ from seven dairy plants of different capacities processing 36% of raw milk in Serbia. A total of 29 life cycle assessment calculations have been performed to identify and quantify the environmental impacts from a cradle-to-grave perspective covering three subsystems: 'dairy farm', 'dairy plant' and 'waste and waste water treatment'. Structured surveys in selected dairies have been conducted in order to collect life cycle inventory input data. Six environmental impact potentials were calculated in this study: global warming potential, acidification potential, eutrophication potential, ozone layer depletion, photochemical smog and human toxicity. The focus of the research was put on global warming potential, acidification po- tential and eutrophication potential.

As a general finding, this study confirmed previous research that the largest contributor to the envi- ronmental profile is the raw milk production at dairy farms. Contributions of the dairy processing plants are mainly due to energy requirement and inputs of goods at the dairy gate. Mitigation options for optimization of environmental impacts rely on the choice of the production/packaging portfolio, energy fuel profile, water optimization and waste management.

5: Dairy products play a significant role in human diet due to their uniqueness, desirability, economical and nutritional value. How- ever, these products are associated with environmental impacts and large amounts of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions when compared to food of similar nutritive content (Milani et al., 2011).

Typical environmental performance indicators in the dairy sector are product yield, water consumption, water-to-milk ratio, water reuse, energy consumption, energy-to-milk ratio, waste water discharge, solid waste performance and usage of chemicals. All indicators are calculated per 1 kg of final product or per 1 L of raw milk as outlined in European Integrated Pollution Prevention and Control document for the food, drink and milk industries (IPPC) (IPPC, 2006), and in the United Nations Environment Program (UNEP) document UNEP (2004). Along with presented environ- mental indicators, footprints are often used as quantitative mea- surements of human activities in terms of environmental, social, and economic dimensions (Cucek et al., 2012). Proper evaluation of
environmental and socio-economic impacts resulting from indus- trial processes is very important (Zhao et al., 2006).

Environmental indicators and performance measurements are key issues to prevent the consequences of unsustainable resource utilization and adverse environmental impacts (Herva et al., 2011). Two International Dairy Federation (IDF) Bulletins analyzed envi- ronmental indicators in dairy industry and the most of presented data were related to developed countries and regions (Scandinavia, United Kingdom (UK), countries from the European Union (EU), United States, Australia, New Zealand, Brazil and India) (IDF, 2009, 2010). Different methodologies and the ways of collecting data often lead to certain inconsistencies with no possibilities to compare them. In this context, there is a constant need to combine the simplicity required at company level, and the scientific de- mands and transparency necessary to make reliable analysis of environmental performance data (Herva et al., 2011). One of the most often used approaches in environmental per- formance analysis is life-cycle assessment (LCA). This methodology has been used as a tool to identify hot spots in the production chain that may introduce opportunities for lowering environmental im- pacts and improving efficiency and profitability (Eide, 2002). As outlined in IDF (2009), approximately 85% of the GHG and 40% of energy consumption are linked to the dairy farms. Environmental impact of the farms is mainly associated with the feed production,

while GHG emissions are associated with the farming of cows. Although dairy processing contributes less to the overall environ- mental issues, process improvements and decrease of environ- mental impacts are very desirable (Milani et al., 2011).

The overview of recent LCA studies in dairy sector, emphasizing system boundaries and environmental impacts, is presented in Table 1. The global warming, acidification and eutrophication poten- tials were the most common indicators related to the environmental impact of dairy sector in presented studies. IDF guide considers them as main damage categories; global warming being the major cause of climate change and acidification and eutrophication affecting ecosystem quality. This guide points the importance of analyzing consumption of resources, both energy and water (IDF, 2009).

According to the Serbian Chamber of Commerce, food produc- tion participates with 6.4% in the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) of the Republic of Serbia. The dairy sector is among the leading food sectors. Joint with agriculture, with the total share of 17.0%, food industry plays a significant role in the economy of Serbia (Yearbook, 2011). According to official data for 2011 and 2012, total cow milk production in Serbia was around 1,500 million liters (ML) per annum out of which approximately 700 ML (near 50%) were further delivered and processed in 201 dairy plants of different capacities (Analysis, 2012). Latest governmental survey indicated that existing

Serbian dairies can be categorized, according to their daily pro- cessing capacity, into the following groups: (i) plants with capacity over 20,000 L of milk/d, (ii) plants with capacity below 20,000 L of milk/d and (iii) small artisanal dairies with daily capacity below 3000 L of milk/d. The first group of dairies, that represents 15% of the total number of plants, contributes with more than 80% in total raw milk processing (Analysis, 2012).

Environmental impacts of the Serbian food industry, including dairy processing, have not been studied yet. To the best of our knowledge and available data, this paper presents the first life cycle inventory and environmental consequences identification of dairy products in this part of Europe.

Due to importance of studying environmental impacts, the objective of this paper was to assess the environmental perfor- mance of the production of six types of dairy products (liquid pasteurized and Ultra-high temperature (UHT) milk, yoghurt, cream, butter and cheese) from seven dairy plants. A total of 29 life cycle assessment calculations have been performed.

15: When the objective of the research has been identified, the selected functional unit (FU), which confers a reference to which inputs and outputs are referred to, was set as 1 kg of final product to the customer (IDF, 2009). Inputs for the LCA calculations included raw milk, fresh water, fossil fuels, packaging materials and cleaning agents, while outputs covered final products, waste and waste water discharge. Energy consumption analyzed in this study covered electrical and thermal energy used for processing in the plant. Activities taken into consideration were transportation of raw milk, packaging materials and cleaning agents to a plant, and all processing activities within a plant.
16: The system boundaries covered three subsystems 'dairy farm', 'dairy plant' and 'waste and waste water treatment'.

Subsystem 1 - dairy farm: raw milk is the main ingredient in the production of dairy products. Due to the fact that there is no available data covering environmental impact of the raw milk production in Serbia, recommended average values of Global Warming Potential (GWP), Acidification Potential (AP) and Eutro- phication Potential (EP) for the production of 1 kg of raw milk were used as outlined in (IDF, 2009) based on 25 different studies. Average energy content of raw milk in Serbia is 3.7 fat and 3.2 % true protein content (Petrovic et al., 2006).

Subsystem 2 - dairy plant: This subsystem included all activities which take place in a plant, from raw milk reception, through dairy processing, packaging and storage. The transport of raw milk from farm to dairy plant as well as transport of packaging materials and cleaning agents were included in this subsystem and used in the calculations.

Seven Serbian dairy plants (referred as DP) were included in this study. According to their total annual input, these dairy plants represent 36% of total raw milk processed annually in dairy in- dustry in the Republic of Serbia. Regarding daily processing ca- pacity, two dairy plants were categorized into the first group (>20,000 L of milk/d), four into the second group (3000-20,000 L of milk/d) and one represents an artisanal dairy plant with very small capacity (<3.000 L of milk/d) (Analysis, 2012).

In selected dairy plants, raw milk was processed into one or several of the following products: liquid milk (pasteurized and UHT milk), yoghurt (liquid and solid varieties of yoghurt), cream (sour cream), butter and different kinds of cheeses (rennet and acid
coagulated cheeses). The profiles of the dairy plants are presented in Table 2.

Quality characteristics of dairy products produced in seven dairy plants are in compliance with national regulations (Regulation, 2010a) and Codex Alimentarius published by Food and Agricul- ture Organization (FAO) (FAO and WHO, 2011).
Subsystem 3 - waste treatment: this subsystem included issues related to waste management of solid waste and treatment of waste water.
Fig. 1 presents a generic model of system boundaries of the dairy products production life cycle used in this research.
The production of additional ingredients which are used in the dairy products was considered as out of the system boundaries due to their small quantities.

Transport of final products to retail as well as final consumption of dairy products (i.e. consumer purchasing, food storage and preparation at home, food waste and waste handling of packaging) were not included in this assessment since these data were not available.

19: In order to collect data necessary for the calculations, a struc- tured questionnaire was developed, based on the guidelines pub- lished by IDF (2010). It was sent to the selected dairy plants in advance giving them time to gather data. Plants were requested to provide data for the year 2011, on a 'whole of factory basis'. During on-site visits that took place in the period March-July 2012, pre- sented information were discussed with the responsible personnel in order to check uniformity of metric units of the data.
20:
<list>Raw data obtained from the dairy plants, were the main con- stituents of the foreground life cycle inventory data for Subsystem 2. For the purpose of processing data from 'whole of factory basis' to a 'product basis', physic-chemical and mass allocation of inputs and outputs (water consumption, waste water discharge, use of acid and alkaline cleaners, and consumption of electric and thermal energy) were used (Feitz et al., 2007). The percent of the individual product allocation was calculated by multiplying the annual output by its specific allocation factor (AF) reported by Feitz et al. (2007), and then dividing by the sum of all products produced multiplied by its specific AFs.

23: Classification and characterization stages were undertaken in this study, as defined in the life cycle impact assessment stage of the standardized LCA methodology (ISO, 2006). Normalization and weighting were not considered as these data do not provide added value to the objective of this study. Environmental impact poten- tials calculated in this study were as follows: global warming po- tential (GWP), acidification potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP), ozone layer depletion (ODP), photochemical smog (PS) and human toxicity (HT). CCaLC model was used for processing of the inventories (CCaLC, 2013). Results of the environmental impact assessment calculated per 1 kg of produced dairy product are shown in Table 5.

Results indicated that environmental impacts vary in observed dairies. For milks (both pasteurized and UHT), GWP results were between 1.24 and 1.67 kg CO2 eq. Similar results were presented in studies from other researchers. AP for milks is higher than in Fan- tin's research (Fantin et al., 2012) but lower than in the research of González (González-García et al., 2013b). EP results are similar to those presented in two mentioned studies. Yoghurt was produced in six of the dairies and GWP was in the range from 1.42 to 2.63 kg CO2 eq. This is slightly higher compared to results from González- García et al. (2012). AP and EP are lower compared to the same research.

Regarding the other group of products (cream, butter and cheese), in most countries quality characteristics vary in terms of moisture and fat content, and therefor this limits the comparison. GWP index for Portuguese mature cheese was 7.49 kg CO2 eq (González-García et al., 2013a) while for the Galician cheese it was 10.44 kg CO2 eq (González-García et al., 2013c). Our results were in the range between 6.73 and 9.47 kg CO2 eq (Table 5). The results from our study for AP and EP are lower than presented for the two cheeses while ODP is higher. Environmental impacts of butter are much higher compared to results from the UK, Denmark and France (Nilsson et al., 2010). PS and HT are presented in Table 5, but were not discussed since LCA methodology used by other authors included different environmental impacts.

The relative contributions of each subsystem and each impact category were calculated for all products and dairy plants (data not shown). The results of impact category allowed the identification of subsystems with the highest environmental loads. According to these results it is obvious that the production of raw milk (the main ingredient) at the dairy farm (Subsystem 1) significantly contrib- utes to the environmental impacts. The main reason is that the activities carried out on the dairy farm considerably contributed to the emission of NH3, NO3 - and CH4 derived from the manure production and management and enteric fermentation, as well as NOx and SO2 emissions from combustion of fuels in the agricultural machinery. Off-farm emissions are related to non-renewable en- ergy consumption, mainly for fertilizer production and various transports (IDF, 2009). Most of the articles and studies confirmed that 'subsystem 1' has remarkable contribution in most of the environmental impacts. In cheese production, subsystem 1 for

Eide confirmed that agri- cultural production was found to be of significance for all analyzed environmental parameters in the LCA of milk production (Eide, 2002). Bartl confirms that in the highlands of Peru, livestock and the cultivation of the concentrate ingredients were the main sources for gases contributing to global warming (Bartl et al., 2011). IDF guide which included 25 studies, states that the largest source of GHG emissions is from dairy farming (IDF, 2009). Environmental impact poten- tials calculated in this study were as follows: global warming po- tential (GWP), acidification potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP), ozone layer depletion (ODP), photochemical smog (PS) and human toxicity (HT). CCaLC model was used for processing of the inventories (CCaLC, 2013). Results of the environmental impact assessment calculated per 1 kg of produced dairy product are shown in Table 5.

Results indicated that environmental impacts vary in observed dairies. For milks (both pasteurized and UHT), GWP results were between 1.24 and 1.67 kg CO2 eq. Similar results were presented in studies from other researchers. AP for milks is higher than in Fan- tin's research (Fantin et al., 2012) but lower than in the research of González (González-García et al., 2013b). EP results are similar to those presented in two mentioned studies. Yoghurt was produced in six of the dairies and GWP was in the range from 1.42 to 2.63 kg CO2 eq. This is slightly higher compared to results from González- García et al. (2012). AP and EP are lower compared to the same research.

Regarding the other group of products (cream, butter and cheese), in most countries quality characteristics vary in terms of moisture and fat content, and therefor this limits the comparison. GWP index for Portuguese mature cheese was 7.49 kg CO2 eq (González-García et al., 2013a) while for the Galician cheese it was 10.44 kg CO2 eq (González-García et al., 2013c). Our results were in the range between 6.73 and 9.47 kg CO2 eq (Table 5). The results from our study for AP and EP are lower than presented for the two cheeses while ODP is higher. Environmental impacts of butter are much higher compared to results from the UK, Denmark and France (Nilsson et al., 2010). PS and HT are presented in Table 5, but were not discussed since LCA methodology used by other authors included different environmental impacts.

The relative contributions of each subsystem and each impact category were calculated for all products and dairy plants (data not shown). The results of impact category allowed the identification of subsystems with the highest environmental loads. According to these results it is obvious that the production of raw milk (the main ingredient) at the dairy farm (Subsystem 1) significantly contrib- utes to the environmental impacts. The main reason is that the activities carried out on the dairy farm considerably contributed to the emission of NH3, NO3 - and CH4 derived from the manure production and management and enteric fermentation, as well as NOx and SO2 emissions from combustion of fuels in the agricultural machinery. Off-farm emissions are related to non-renewable en- ergy consumption, mainly for fertilizer production and various transports (IDF, 2009). Most of the articles and studies confirmed that 'subsystem 1' has remarkable contribution in most of the environmental impacts. In cheese production, subsystem 1 for
Table 3 Global inventory for the production of dairy products per 1 kg of final product (Subsystems 2 and 3).

GWP, AP and EP contributes over 80%, compared to other sub- systems (González-García et al., 2013a). Eide confirmed that agri- cultural production was found to be of significance for all analyzed environmental parameters in the LCA of milk production (Eide, 2002). Bartl confirms that in the highlands of Peru, livestock and the cultivation of the concentrate ingredients were the main sources for gases contributing to global warming (Bartl et al., 2011). IDF guide which included 25 studies, states that the largest source of GHG emissions is from dairy farming (IDF, 2009). Same guide points that acidification score is more than 90% dominated by dairy farming and that the main contributor to eutrophication is usage of fertilizers on the farms.

Relative contributions of these two subsystems for milks and yoghurt are much higher than for products such as butter, cream and cheese which utilize more raw milk per 1 kg of final product.

Acidification is also dominated by the farm, as it is coming from nitrogen fertilizers, manure degradation and nitrification-denitri- fication processes in both soils and manure (IDF, 2009). This envi- ronmental impact category measures the impact of the release of acidifying substances (such as sulfur and nitrogen oxides) into ecosystems (Jan et al., 2012).

The main contributor to eutrophication is the use of mineral and organic fertilizers as well as production of fodder causing over- fertilization of water and soil. It leads to degradation of the envi- ronment, less desirable water quality and usage of the water body (Chau, 2007). Eutrophication covers all potential impacts of high environmental levels of macronutrients, in particular nitrogen and phosphorus (van der Werf et al., 2009).

32: Classification and characterization stages were undertaken in this study, as defined in the life cycle impact assessment stage of the standardized LCA methodology (ISO, 2006). Normalization and weighting were not considered as these data do not provide added value to the objective of this study. Environmental impact poten- tials calculated in this study were as follows: global warming po- tential (GWP), acidification potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP), ozone layer depletion (ODP), photochemical smog (PS) and human toxicity (HT). CCaLC model was used for processing of the inventories (CCaLC, 2013). Results of the environmental impact assessment calculated per 1 kg of produced dairy product are shown in Table 5.

Results indicated that environmental impacts vary in observed dairies. For milks (both pasteurized and UHT), GWP results were between 1.24 and 1.67 kg CO2 eq. Similar results were presented in studies from other researchers. AP for milks is higher than in Fan- tin's research (Fantin et al., 2012) but lower than in the research of González (González-García et al., 2013b). EP results are similar to those presented in two mentioned studies. Yoghurt was produced in six of the dairies and GWP was in the range from 1.42 to 2.63 kg CO2 eq. This is slightly higher compared to results from González- García et al. (2012). AP and EP are lower compared to the same research.

Regarding the other group of products (cream, butter and cheese), in most countries quality characteristics vary in terms of moisture and fat content, and therefor this limits the comparison. GWP index for Portuguese mature cheese was 7.49 kg CO2 eq (González-García et al., 2013a) while for the Galician cheese it was 10.44 kg CO2 eq (González-García et al., 2013c). Our results were in the range between 6.73 and 9.47 kg CO2 eq (Table 5). The results from our study for AP and EP are lower than presented for the two cheeses while ODP is higher. Environmental impacts of butter are much higher compared to results from the UK, Denmark and France (Nilsson et al., 2010). PS and HT are presented in Table 5, but were not discussed since LCA methodology used by other authors included different environmental impacts.

The relative contributions of each subsystem and each impact category were calculated for all products and dairy plants (data not shown). The results of impact category allowed the identification of subsystems with the highest environmental loads. According to these results it is obvious that the production of raw milk (the main ingredient) at the dairy farm (Subsystem 1) significantly contrib- utes to the environmental impacts. The main reason is that the activities carried out on the dairy farm considerably contributed to the emission of NH3, NO3 - and CH4 derived from the manure production and management and enteric fermentation, as well as NOx and SO2 emissions from combustion of fuels in the agricultural machinery. Off-farm emissions are related to non-renewable en- ergy consumption, mainly for fertilizer production and various transports (IDF, 2009). 33: Considering a cradle-to-gate perspective, the results obtained in this study are in accordance with other LCA studies where different dairy products were analyzed. In the studies where 'subsystem 1' was included (Table 1), the activities carried out at the farm and related to the production of raw milk show the highest environ- mental impacts expressed as GWP, AP and EP. Although the main environmental hotspot for most of the impact categories is the raw milk production at the farm, it is also important to analyze the impacts of other stages in the life-cycle of dairy products.

Three categories involved in the dairy factory have been extracted, excluding raw milk production as the main input. Results are presented in these categories since activities, inputs and out- puts are controlled by the dairy plants (Table 6). The first category, 'Input and packaging category' consists of water, packaging mate- rials and cleaning agents used in the dairy plants. All types of en- ergy needed for both transportation as well as dairy processing were grouped under second category 'Energy'. Third category named 'Output' considered waste and waste water.
'Energy' was identified as an influential contributor to GWP in the plants producing pasteurized milk (Table 6). The main reason is
Table 5 Environmental impact assessment results associated with the production of 1 kg of various dairy products from seven dairy plants.

due to fossil carbon dioxide emissions derived from the combustion of fuels in transportation trucks and from electricity production, which mostly depends on non-renewable sources (González- García et al., 2013a). However, in two plants, (DP1 and DP7) re- sults showed that 'Energy' was not the main contributors to GWP. Instead, 'Input and packaging materials' showed the highest impact on GWP, with over 50% in DP7. Transportation of raw milk was with the lowest impact due to the fact that dairy farms and processing plants are in close proximity to each other. Outputs were below 20%, except in DP2 where it was near 25%.

UHT milk was produced in two dairy plants. The results showed that 'Energy' contributes with more than 50%. Among the three liquid products, as outlined in Fig. 2, yoghurt production contrib- utes the most to GWP (up to 40%). Except in the case of DP2, 'En- ergy' was the main contributor to GWP. It is important to note that DP2 has a farm within the dairy plant so no transportation of raw milk was reported.

Within the other three groups of products (cream, butter, cheese), 'Energy' prevails in all dairies, except for cream in DP6. Energy efficiency in a dairy factory is closely related to the product. The production of liquid milk or yoghurt requires less energy supply than the cheese manufacturing showing highest energy demand (Berlin et al., 2008).

Energy consumption has its impact in dairy processing and packaging. The dominant form of energy which is used in dairy plants are steam (both product and non-product contact), hot water, heated and/or dehumidified drier air, chilled water, chilled air and electricity. Refrigeration and compressed air are generally produced using electricity (IDF, 2005). Factors that influence on the rate of energy consumptions in dairy plants are numerous and include production location (climate), plant size (capacity and layout) and age, plant utilization rate, level of automation and electric/thermal energy (Xu and Flapper, 2009). The type and amounts of energy depend on the technological processes as well as types of products manufactured in dairy plants (UNEP, 2004).

Fig. 2. Relative contributions and standard deviation (in %) of subsystems 2 and 3 to Global Warming Potential (GWP), excluding raw milk production (main input). Acro- nyms: PM - pasteurized milk, UH - UHT milk, YO yoghurt; CR - cream, BU - butter, CH - cheese. Subsystems 2 and 3: dairy plant and waste outputs.

Dairy processing demands both electric and thermal energy. Surveyed dairy plants use electricity and fossil fuels - particularly diesel fuels, liquid petrol gas and natural gas, while DP3 also re- ported usage of bio fuels, particularly wood. Proportion of elec- tricity and thermal use varies from dairy to dairy and is usually from 20 to 30% till 80-70% (IPPC, 2006; UNEP, 2004). Proportion of the two types of energies varied in the dairies from 3 to 61% for electric and 97-39% for thermal energy. This is due to the fact that some of the dairy plants have highly modernized equipment (DP4), while some of them have very old machines and equipment (DP2). Also companies reported different types of fuels used for produc- tion of thermal energy (Table 4). Different types of transportation (transport of milk from dairy farms, packaging materials and cleaning agents to the plants) were not energy consuming since transportation distances were relatively short. Energy used for transportation and maintaining the cold chain was fossil fuel.

Factors that influence on the rate of energy consumptions in dairy plants are numerous and include production location (climate), plant size (capacity and layout) and age, plant utilization rate, level of automation and electric/thermal energy (Xu and Flapper, 2009). Dairy processing demands both electric and thermal energy. Surveyed dairy plants use electricity and fossil fuels - particularly diesel fuels, liquid petrol gas and natural gas, while DP3 also re- ported usage of bio fuels, particularly wood. Proportion of elec- tricity and thermal use varies from dairy to dairy and is usually from 20 to 30% till 80-70% (IPPC, 2006; UNEP, 2004). Proportion of the two types of energies varied in the dairies from 3 to 61% for electric and 97-39% for thermal energy. This is due to the fact that some of the dairy plants have highly modernized equipment (DP4), while some of them have very old machines and equipment (DP2). Also companies reported different types of fuels used for produc- tion of thermal energy (Table 4). Different types of transportation (transport of milk from dairy farms, packaging materials and cleaning agents to the plants) were not energy consuming since transportation distances were relatively short. Energy used for transportation and maintaining the cold chain was fossil fuel.

In five dairy plants, there was no recovery/reuse ratio reported. Most companies had no improvement plans in recovery/ reuse of water due to the lack of knowledge. Regarding water saving programs, there are numerous opportunities in minimizing water consumption in analyzed dairy plants such as optimization of water flow rate, efficient process control, reduction of water for cleaning and utilities, change-over of production schedule, water reuse and recycling.

Acronyms: PM - pasteurized milk, UH - UHT milk, YO - yoghurt; CR - cream, BU - butter, CH - cheese. Subsystems 2 & 3: Dairy plant and waste outputs.

Acronyms: PM - pasteurized milk, UH - UHT milk, YO - yoghurt; CR - cream, BU - butter, CH cheese. Subsystems 2 & 3: Dairy plant and waste outputs.

maintain the quality of the milk-derived products, and for the operation of utilities such as cooling water and steam production (UNEP, 2004). Water consumption calculated per kg of final product is an indicator of efficient use of water and represents a combina- tion of condensate reuse and best cleaning practices (Milani et al., 2011).

Adequate cleaning and sanitation of all dairy equipment with approved chemicals is essential in dairy processing since water alone is inadequate. This activity is necessary to prevent accumu- lation of undesirable micro-organisms in the equipment, develop- ment of bad smells, possible corrosion of metal parts due to lactic acid, mold growth leading to mold contamination in the product and contamination of the product with pathogens. The method of conventional alkaline/acidic cleaning with sodium hydroxide and nitric acid was observed in the dairies, as outlined by (Berlin et al., 2007). All dairies reported a cleaning-in-place (CIP) system.

This activity in dairy processing is another area of continual examination, optimization and innovation of different chemicals and technologies used to avoid solute wastewater discharge (Milani et al., 2011). The main improvements are in the dosing system of chemicals based on conductivity of solutions; heating system for increasing the effectiveness of solutions and a pumping system for recycling in the CIP (Özbay and Demirer, 2007). Optimization of product change-over minimizes use of cleaning agents and use of water for cleaning (Berlin et al., 2008). This improvement also af- fects quality of discharged waste water and consequently GWP.

Production of packaging materials consumes non-renewable energy for the extraction of materials and their transportation to dairy plants. Analyzed dairy plants reported a set of different packaging materials for various products, mainly Polyethylene, Polyvinyl chloride and Aluminum films. Dairy plants face increasing pressure to develop and use packaging that reduce the consumption of resources, enable reuse or recycling, and minimize landfill disposal (UNEP, 2004). Recycling packaging materials have a lower environmental impact than disposal to landfills or incinera- tion plants, for all materials and sizes compared so optimization of packaging as well as improved waste management can be identi- fied as a direction of improvement (Meneses et al., 2012).

Efficient use of various processing energy forms as well as choice of energy sources with lower emission can make a signifi- cant contribution to the minimization of energy consumption in dairy plants. The energy-saving actions should include cross- cutting measures that are used in food processing (e.g., variable frequency control, leak repair in steam systems, and refrigeration

systems). Additional measures include changing cleaning cycle, reducing sterilization time, shutting down machines outside working hours, and reducing cleaning water temperature (Xu and Flapper, 2009).
Environmental impact related to transportation energy can be improved by using new vehicles with better air emission perfor- mance as well as by optimizing transportation routes.

Solid waste generated by dairy plants included packaging waste such as cardboard, cartons, paper and plastic, and organic waste such as sludge and reject product (UNEP, 2004). Also, optimization of product changeovers results in decreased total amount of waste, (Berlin et al., 2007). Latest implementation of several environ- mental laws primarily for waste management (Regulation, 2010b) and managing of waste packaging (Regulation, 2009) resulted in more precise monitoring of waste as well as development of waste management plans in Serbia. Further handling of waste in diary plants should focus on four directions - recycling, incineration, composting and disposal at landfills (IPPC, 2006).

Dairy factories produce large amounts of wastewater with a high organic load (González-García et al., 2013a). However, wastewater composition differs depending of product type. For example, 'white' products such as milk, cream and yoghurt have lower impact compared to 'yellow' products such as butter and cheese (IPPC, 2006). Separating wastewater streams on the basis of quality can also reduce the load on wastewater treatment systems, and give opportunities for reuse that would not arise if the streams were combined (e.g. separating whey streams for further treat- ment) (UNEP, 2004).

Some wastewater streams are relatively clean and can be recy- cled or reused onsite. Service water, steam condensate and heating water are sources for potential reuse (Özbay and Demirer, 2007). Improved CIP systems and adequate product removal prior to the start of the CIP cycle are directions of decreasing the quantity of wastewater as well as prevention of large quantities of product entering wastewater. Waste water has an extremely variable composition, depending on the technology applied and whether whey is segregated (IPPC, 2006). In five of the dairies that produce cheese, whey was not segregated.

Separating wastewater streams on the basis of quality can also reduce the load on wastewater treatment systems, and give opportunities for reuse that would not arise if the streams were combined (e.g. separating whey streams for further treat- ment) (UNEP, 2004).

Some wastewater streams are relatively clean and can be recy- cled or reused onsite. Service water, steam condensate and heating water are sources for potential reuse (Özbay and Demirer, 2007). Improved CIP systems and adequate product removal prior to the start of the CIP cycle are directions of decreasing the quantity of wastewater as well as prevention of large quantities of product entering wastewater. Waste water has an extremely variable composition, depending on the technology applied and whether whey is segregated (IPPC, 2006). In five of the dairies that produce cheese, whey was not segregated.

In five dairy plants, there was no recovery/reuse ratio reported. Most companies had no improvement plans in recovery/ reuse of water due to the lack of knowledge. Regarding water saving programs, there are numerous opportunities in minimizing water consumption in analyzed dairy plants such as optimization of water flow rate, efficient process control, reduction of water for cleaning and utilities, change-over of production schedule, water reuse and recycling.

Water consumption calculated per kg of final product is an indicator of efficient use of water and represents a combina- tion of condensate reuse and best cleaning practices (Milani et al., 2011).

Adequate cleaning and sanitation of all dairy equipment with approved chemicals is essential in dairy processing since water alone is inadequate. This activity is necessary to prevent accumu- lation of undesirable micro-organisms in the equipment, develop- ment of bad smells, possible corrosion of metal parts due to lactic acid, mold growth leading to mold contamination in the product and contamination of the product with pathogens. The method of conventional alkaline/acidic cleaning with sodium hydroxide and nitric acid was observed in the dairies, as outlined by (Berlin et al., 2007). All dairies reported a cleaning-in-place (CIP) system.

This activity in dairy processing is another area of continual examination, optimization and innovation of different chemicals and technologies used to avoid solute wastewater discharge (Milani et al., 2011). The main improvements are in the dosing system of chemicals based on conductivity of solutions; heating system for increasing the effectiveness of solutions and a pumping system for recycling in the CIP (Özbay and Demirer, 2007). Optimization of product change-over minimizes use of cleaning agents and use of water for cleaning (Berlin et al., 2008). This improvement also af- fects quality of discharged waste water and consequently GWP.

Production of packaging materials consumes non-renewable energy for the extraction of materials and their transportation to dairy plants. Analyzed dairy plants reported a set of different packaging materials for various products, mainly Polyethylene, Polyvinyl chloride and Aluminum films. Dairy plants face increasing pressure to develop and use packaging that reduce the consumption of resources, enable reuse or recycling, and minimize landfill disposal (UNEP, 2004). Recycling packaging materials have a lower environmental impact than disposal to landfills or incinera- tion plants, for all materials and sizes compared so optimization of packaging as well as improved waste management can be identi- fied as a direction of improvement (Meneses et al., 2012).

Efficient use of various processing energy forms as well as choice of energy sources with lower emission can make a signifi- cant contribution to the minimization of energy consumption in dairy plants. The energy-saving actions should include cross- cutting measures that are used in food processing (e.g., variable frequency control, leak repair in steam systems, and refrigeration

systems). Additional measures include changing cleaning cycle, reducing sterilization time, shutting down machines outside working hours, and reducing cleaning water temperature (Xu and Flapper, 2009).
Environmental impact related to transportation energy can be improved by using new vehicles with better air emission perfor- mance as well as by optimizing transportation routes.

Solid waste generated by dairy plants included packaging waste such as cardboard, cartons, paper and plastic, and organic waste such as sludge and reject product (UNEP, 2004). Also, optimization of product changeovers results in decreased total amount of waste, (Berlin et al., 2007). Latest implementation of several environ- mental laws primarily for waste management (Regulation, 2010b) and managing of waste packaging (Regulation, 2009) resulted in more precise monitoring of waste as well as development of waste management plans in Serbia. Further handling of waste in diary plants should focus on four directions - recycling, incineration, composting and disposal at landfills (IPPC, 2006).

46: Level of innovativeness related to improving environmental impact of Serbian dairies, depends greatly on the type, capacity of the plants and company's environmental awareness. Accordingly, large companies are characterized with a better control of all food processes and are constantly working on environmental friendly dairy processing. On the other side, small capacity dairies are characterized by low level of innovativeness in terms of environ- mental protection and improvement of dairy processing, mainly due to lack of financial assets and knowledge. Directions for im- provements in Serbian dairies are different depending on the product portfolio, capacity and overall environmental awareness. Potential alternatives suitable to reduce the contributions to the environmental profile over the entire life cycle of the dairy prod- ucts in Serbian plants could be summarized in three directions. First dimension comprises conservation efforts to reduce fuel and elec- tricity use within the dairy product life cycle which could have a broad beneficial impact, both economic, through cost savings, and environmental, due to the reduction in emissions (Kim et al., 2013). Many small processing facilities combined into larger operations may result in energy efficiency per functional unit (Milani et al., 2011). In line with this, increase of processing capacities can result in additional energy savings (Wojdalski et al., 2008). Second direction is focused on minimizing dairy product losses at all pro- cessing stages in the dairy plants. This has a significant potential to

50: Level of innovativeness related to improving environmental impact of Serbian dairies, depends greatly on the type, capacity of the plants and company's environmental awareness. Accordingly, large companies are characterized with a better control of all food processes and are constantly working on environmental friendly dairy processing. On the other side, small capacity dairies are characterized by low level of innovativeness in terms of environ- mental protection and improvement of dairy processing, mainly due to lack of financial assets and knowledge. Directions for im- provements in Serbian dairies are different depending on the product portfolio, capacity and overall environmental awareness. Potential alternatives suitable to reduce the contributions to the environmental profile over the entire life cycle of the dairy prod- ucts in Serbian plants could be summarized in three directions. First dimension comprises conservation efforts to reduce fuel and elec- tricity use within the dairy product life cycle which could have a broad beneficial impact, both economic, through cost savings, and environmental, due to the reduction in emissions (Kim et al., 2013). Many small processing facilities combined into larger operations may result in energy efficiency per functional unit (Milani et al., 2011). In line with this, increase of processing capacities can result in additional energy savings (Wojdalski et al., 2008). 51: The dairy sector is one of the most important and dynamic food manufacturing sectors in Serbia. The study covered the life cycle assessment from a dairy plant perspective and displayed, for the first time, environmental impacts of six types of dairy products from seven dairy plants. Although most of the obtained data are in line with existing literature, there is space for improvement and reduction of the environmental impacts of dairy industry in Serbia. All improve- ment options need to be supported by and based upon new water and energy information, processing technologies, product portfolio and the local economy.

This study confirmed previous researches that the largest contributor to the environmental profile is raw milk production at dairy farms. Contributions of the dairy processing plants are mainly due to energy requirement and inputs of goods at the dairy gate. Relative contributions are different depending on the dairy prod- ucts type. Climate change and cumulative energy demand impacts are closely linked to fuel consumption and energy demands. In dairy plants, mitigation options for optimization of environmental indicators rely on the choice of the production/packaging portfolio,
energy fuel profile and water optimization which is based on eco- nomic and environmental indicators.

Promotion of environmental-friendly chemicals can enable effective and efficient use of chemicals. Considering waste, it is expected that new waste management legislation will increase the awareness concerning waste segregation, development of waste management plans and better environmental performance. For the majority of the improvement options, the overall uncertainty on the environmental improvement is dominated by the assumption of the degree to which the improvement option can be implemented.

Limitations of the research stem from the use of data from a limited number of dairy plants and the current results should not be generalized. Quality of the results and calculations is directly linked to raw data obtained from company management.

Given the great technological and other differences within the dairies, as well as specific characteristics of dairy products in Serbia, more research is necessary to determine if similar results would be derived across other dairies in Serbia and this part of Europe.
